rationale: CC1.1 (COSO P1: commitment to integrity/ethics) is narrower than ISO 5.1 which requires a full information security policy set covering all ISMS objectives, not only ethics.
Both require top management to demonstrate leadership and commitment to information security, including defining policy, roles, and accountability.
CC1.1 ethics/integrity commitment provides the governance context for ISO 6.1 organisational roles definitions, but the two address different control planes.
CC1.2 (COSO P2: independent board oversight) maps to ISO 5.2 leadership/top-management requirements, though ISO does not explicitly require independent non-executive board review.
CC1.2's board-level segregation of oversight duties partially aligns with ISO 6.1 roles/responsibilities but ISO covers broader ISMS role assignment.
CC1.3 (COSO P3: management structure/reporting lines) is substantively equivalent to ISO 5.3 requiring defined organisational roles, responsibilities and authorities for ISMS.
ISO 6.1 defines information security roles and responsibilities matching CC1.3 requirement to establish structure and reporting.
CC1.4 (COSO P4: commitment to competence) maps directly to ISO 6.3 (Awareness) and ISO 7.2 (Competence) requiring staff capability.
ISO 7.2 Competence requires organisations to determine necessary competence, ensure persons are qualified and take action — matching CC1.4's commitment to attract/retain competent staff.
CC1.5 (COSO P5: accountability for internal control) is equivalent to ISO 5.3 which mandates assigned accountability for ISMS performance.
ISO 6.1 defines roles and responsibilities within the ISMS, directly supporting the accountability-assignment requirement in CC1.5.
CC2.1 (COSO P13: internal information quality) requires management to obtain/generate relevant quality information; ISO 5.1 policy communicates requirements but is broader in scope.
ISO 7.4 (Communication) requires determining what, when, how and to whom information security information is communicated — directly equivalent to CC2.1's internal communication requirement.
CC2.2 (COSO P14: internal communication) requires communication of responsibilities for internal control; ISO 7.4 mandates structured internal information security communication.
CC2.2's internal awareness requirement is a subset of ISO 6.3 Awareness which extends to all persons working under the organisation's control.

CC2.3 (COSO P15: external communication) requires communicating with external parties; ISO 7.4 covers communication but focuses primarily on internal audiences and interested parties as defined in 4.2.
CC2.3 external reporting requirements overlap with ISO 5.19 (Information security in supplier relationships) for third-party communications, though ISO is narrower on supplier context.

CC3.1 (COSO P6: specify suitable objectives) maps to ISO 6.1.2 (Information security risk assessment) which requires establishing risk criteria and identifying risks linked to information security objectives.
ISO 6.2 (Information security objectives) requires objectives be measurable and consistent with risk results — directly equivalent to CC3.1 establishing objectives to identify risk.
CC3.2 (COSO P7: identify and analyse risk) maps directly to ISO 6.1.2 risk identification, analysis and evaluation processes.
CC3.3 (COSO P8: assess fraud risk) is a subset-equivalent of ISO 6.1.2 risk assessment; ISO does not distinguish fraud risk specifically but covers all information security risks.
CC3.4 (COSO P9: identify and analyse significant changes) partially maps to ISO 6.1.2 and 8.2 (risk re-assessment) but ISO explicitly requires periodic and change-triggered reassessment.
ISO 8.2 requires conducting information security risk assessments at planned intervals or when significant changes occur — directly equivalent to CC3.4 change-impact analysis requirement.
CC4.1 (COSO P16: select, develop, perform continuous evaluations) maps to ISO 9.1 (Monitoring, measurement, analysis and evaluation) which requires ongoing performance monitoring.
CC4.1 ongoing monitoring activities map to ISO 9.2 (Internal audit) which requires planned, objective evaluation of ISMS conformance.
CC4.2 (COSO P17: evaluate and communicate deficiencies) maps to ISO 9.3 (Management review) which requires management to evaluate findings and determine corrective actions.
ISO 10.1 (Continual improvement) and CC4.2 both require reporting deficiencies upward and ensuring appropriate corrective action — substantively equivalent.
CC5.1 (COSO P10: control activities to mitigate risk) generally aligns with ISO 5.1 policy set but ISO Annex A provides far more granular controls than the COSO principle-level requirement.
ISO 8.3 (Information security risk treatment) requires selecting and implementing controls, which is the exact purpose of CC5.1's control activity selection requirement.
CC5.2 (COSO P11: technology deployment for control activities) focuses on technology controls; ISO 8.3 covers all control types (technical, organisational, people, physical) and is thus a superset.
CC5.2 technology deployment for controls overlaps with ISO 8.1 (Operational planning and control) which governs how risk treatment plans are executed in operations.
CC5.3 (COSO P12: policies and procedures deployment) maps to ISO 6.1 organisational roles and 5.1 policies, both requiring documented policies covering all aspects of control activities.
ISO 7.5 (Documented information) requires maintaining policies and procedures as documented information — directly supporting CC5.3's documented control activities requirement.
CC6.1 (logical and physical access controls) is substantively equivalent to ISO 5.15 (Access control) which defines the access control policy and rules.
CC6.1 user ID management maps directly to ISO 5.16 (Identity management) requiring management of the full identity lifecycle.
CC6.1 password/authentication controls map to ISO 5.17 (Authentication information) covering password management and secret-management requirements.
CC6.1 access provisioning controls map to ISO 5.18 (Access rights) which governs provisioning, review and revocation of access rights.
CC6.1 privileged access restrictions map to ISO 8.2 (Privileged access rights) which requires strict controls over privileged accounts.
CC6.2 (physical access controls at data centres and offices) maps to ISO 7.1 (Physical security perimeters) requiring defined and secured physical boundaries.
CC6.2 physical entry controls map to ISO 7.2 (Physical entry) requiring controls to prevent unauthorised physical access.
CC6.2 securing offices/facilities maps to ISO 7.3 (Securing offices, rooms and facilities).
CC6.3 (access removal on role change/termination) maps to ISO 5.18 Access rights which requires revocation procedures on role change or termination.
CC6.3 access revocation on termination maps to ISO 6.5 (Responsibilities after termination or change of employment) requiring timely revocation.
CC6.4 (physical access restrictions — management of physical access points) maps to ISO 7.1 physical security perimeters.
CC6.4 physical surveillance/monitoring maps to ISO 7.4 (Physical security monitoring) which requires surveillance of premises.
CC6.5 (access rights — logical) requires periodic access review; ISO 5.18 includes review and update of access rights at regular intervals.
CC6.5 secure authentication maps to ISO 8.5 (Secure authentication) covering authentication mechanisms and session management.
CC6.6 (external access / remote access security) maps to ISO 5.14 (Information transfer) and network access controls for external connectivity.
CC6.6 network access control maps to ISO 8.20 (Networks security) which requires segregation and protection of network access.
CC6.6 security of network services maps to ISO 8.21 (Security of network services) covering VPN, firewalls, and service contracts.
CC6.7 (data-at-rest encryption) maps to ISO 8.11 (Data masking) and ISO 8.24 (Use of cryptography), both governing protection of stored sensitive data.
CC6.7 encryption of sensitive data in transit and at rest maps directly to ISO 8.24 (Use of cryptography) which defines cryptographic policy and key management.
CC6.7 data classification prior to encryption maps to ISO 5.12 (Classification of information) which requires information to be classified to determine appropriate protection.
CC6.8 (anti-malware controls) maps to ISO 8.7 (Protection against malware) which requires malware detection and prevention controls.
CC6.8 vulnerability management maps to ISO 8.8 (Management of technical vulnerabilities) covering patching, scanning and remediation.
CC7.1 (detection of anomalies/threats via logging) maps to ISO 8.15 (Logging) which requires event logs capturing security-relevant activities.
CC7.1 monitoring of systems for anomalies maps to ISO 8.16 (Monitoring activities) which requires monitoring of networks, systems and applications.
CC7.1 threat detection maps to ISO 5.7 (Threat intelligence) which requires collecting and analysing information about threats.
CC7.2 (evaluate security events) maps to ISO 5.25 (Assessment and decision on information security events) requiring classification of events.
CC7.2 incident response activation maps to ISO 5.26 (Response to information security incidents) which requires structured incident response procedures.
CC7.2 reporting of security events maps to ISO 6.8 (Information security event reporting) requiring personnel to report events promptly.
CC7.3 (incident response planning) maps to ISO 5.24 (Information security incident management planning and preparation) which requires documented incident management plans.
CC7.3 incident containment and mitigation maps to ISO 5.26 response procedures covering containment, eradication and recovery.
CC7.4 (incident response — notification and communication) maps to ISO 5.26 which requires defined communication procedures during incidents.
CC7.4 learning from incidents maps to ISO 5.27 (Learning from information security incidents) requiring post-incident analysis and improvement.
CC7.5 (post-incident review) is directly equivalent to ISO 5.27 learning from incidents which requires root-cause analysis and preventive action.
CC7.5 recovery activities overlap with ISO 5.29 (Information security during disruption) which addresses continuity during incidents, but ISO 5.29 is scoped to business continuity context.
CC8.1 (change management process) is substantively equivalent to ISO 8.32 (Change management) which requires controlled processes for changes to information processing facilities.
CC8.1 security testing of changes maps to ISO 8.29 (Security testing in development and acceptance) which requires security testing before deploying changes.
CC8.1 secure development lifecycle requirements map to ISO 8.25 (Secure development life cycle) which requires security at all phases of system development.
CC9.1 (risk mitigation through business continuity) maps to ISO 5.30 (ICT readiness for business continuity) which requires ICT continuity planning.
CC9.1 business disruption risk mitigation maps to ISO 5.29 (Information security during disruption) requiring continuity of security controls during adverse events.
CC9.2 (vendor/third-party risk management) maps to ISO 5.19 (Information security in supplier relationships) requiring security requirements in supplier agreements.
CC9.2 addressing security in supplier agreements maps to ISO 5.20 (Addressing information security within supplier agreements).
CC9.2 ICT supply chain security maps to ISO 5.21 (Managing information security in the ICT supply chain).
CC9.2 monitoring of supplier performance maps to ISO 5.22 (Monitoring, review and change management of supplier services).
Availability A1.1 (capacity planning and performance monitoring) maps to ISO 5.30 (ICT readiness for business continuity) requiring capacity and performance planning for continuity.
A1.1 capacity management maps to ISO 8.6 (Capacity management) which requires monitoring of resource use and projections.

Availability A1.2 (environmental protections, backup power, environmental controls) maps to ISO 5.29 and ISO 7.5 (Protecting against physical and environmental threats) and 7.6 (Working in secure areas).
A1.2 environmental protection controls map to ISO 7.5 (Protecting against physical and environmental threats) covering fire, flood, power disruption.
A1.2 data backup requirements map to ISO 8.13 (Information backup) which requires backup policies, testing and retention.
Availability A1.3 (recovery plan testing and disaster recovery) maps to ISO 5.30 ICT readiness requiring BC plan development, testing and maintenance.
A1.3 backup restoration testing is a subset of ISO 8.13 which covers broader backup management including retention, integrity and test procedures.
Confidentiality C1.1 (identification and classification of confidential information) maps to ISO 5.12 (Classification of information) which requires classification scheme and labelling.
C1.1 labelling of confidential information maps to ISO 5.13 (Labelling of information) requiring labelling according to classification scheme.
Confidentiality C1.2 (encryption of confidential data) maps to ISO 8.24 (Use of cryptography) requiring cryptographic controls for sensitive information.
C1.2 confidentiality classification is a subset of ISO 5.12 information classification, which covers all information types not just confidential.
C1.2 data loss prevention controls for confidential data map to ISO 8.12 (Data leakage prevention) which requires technical controls to prevent unauthorised disclosure.

Processing Integrity PI1.1 (completeness, accuracy and validity of data processing) is a subset of ISO 8.25 secure development lifecycle, which covers broader development security including processing integrity.
PI1.2 (testing of completeness and accuracy of outputs) maps to ISO 8.33 (Test information) which requires proper management and protection of test data.
PI1.3 (output reconciliation and error detection) maps to ISO 8.29 (Security testing in development and acceptance) requiring validation of outputs meets specifications.
PI1.4 (processing integrity in system changes) maps to ISO 8.25 (Secure development life cycle) which requires security and integrity controls throughout the SDLC.
PI1.5 (change controls for processing integrity) maps to ISO 8.32 (Change management) which requires documented, tested and approved changes.

Privacy criterion P1 (notice) — commitment to inform data subjects of privacy practices — maps to ISO 5.34 (Privacy and protection of PII) which requires organisations to implement privacy principles.
P1 notice obligations map to ISO 5.31 (Legal, statutory, regulatory and contractual requirements) which requires identifying privacy-related legal obligations.
Privacy P2 (choice and consent) maps to ISO 5.34 privacy controls, though ISO does not prescribe consent mechanisms with the same granularity as P2.
Privacy P3 (collection) limits data collection to declared purposes; ISO 5.34 references OECD privacy principles including purpose specification and collection limitation.
P3.2 authorised collection mechanisms map to ISO 5.34 which requires lawful processing of PII, but ISO relies on ISO 27701 for detailed privacy controls.
Privacy P4 (use, retention, disposal) maps to ISO 5.34, with detailed PII handling governed by ISO 27701 rather than ISO 27001 Annex A directly.
P4.2 data retention and disposal maps to ISO 8.10 (Deletion of information) which requires secure deletion of information when no longer required.
Privacy P5 (access) — data subject access rights — maps to ISO 5.34 privacy principles; detailed subject rights are addressed in ISO 27701.
Privacy P6 (disclosure and notification to third parties) overlaps with ISO 5.19 supplier information security requirements for third-party data sharing scenarios.
P6.2 notification of data breaches to regulators maps to ISO 5.31 legal/regulatory compliance requirements, though ISO does not prescribe specific breach notification timelines.
Privacy P7 (quality) requires accuracy of personal information; ISO 5.34 references data quality as a privacy principle but ISO 27001 does not provide detailed quality controls.
Privacy P8 (monitoring and enforcement) — privacy programme monitoring — maps to ISO 5.34 but ISO 27001 alone lacks the programme-level enforcement granularity; ISO 27701 fills this gap.
